sentences.text: Well, after a few weeks, I contracted [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] typhoid fever.
And then my good friend, [INAUDIBLE], who was also from my hometown, being a son of a doctor, recognized quicker than others.
And he brought an expert who said that I have to be hospitalized in [NON-ENGLISH] Hospital in Vilna.
At that time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father, who was very worried about my health-- and he had something important to discuss with me according to the letter and phone call.
Anyway, so he took the train on June 21st in the evening.
1941, to go to Vilna.

And when he arrived, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the next morning [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were bombs falling on the-- on the railroad station in Vilna and around it, and of course [INAUDIBLE] airport.
And it was the beginning of Barbarossa, the German attack on Poland-- on-- on Russia, on the 22nd of June.
In two days, the Russians run away.

The Soviet government disintegrated, was replaced by the Wehrmacht, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whom the Lithuanians, ignorant of the fact that the Germans sold them out in 1939, regarded as their saviors.
And I spent three very unpleasant weeks in hospital.
And then I was told that around the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the end of-- of June that I'm ready to be discharged.
But I was also warned by the Polish nuns that Germans and Lithuanian activists are arresting Jews or shooting them on sight.
At that time I remembered by heart the phone number of Dainelis and correctly anticipated that he would be back in the service of the Lithuanian police.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I telephoned him.
He was very happy I was alive, asked me to stay where I am.
And he came with his police car to the hospital.
He discharged me and he took me with him to my apartment.
And this, of course, was a proof that he was grateful for what I did for him in-- a half year earlier under the Soviet-- when the Soviets were in power.
And then he simply tried to save me, which he did.
That was the beginning of my quest for survival, which involved afterwards also Professor Stakauskas of Vilna University--
Who we will come to.
--and the village priest.
Well, he stayed in my apartment.
And I told him not to-- not to move.
He-- he had some food with him.
And that will last him a few days.
And on the 30th of June or the 1st of July, I-- I came home.
So he was a week in my apartment.
I told him not to go out and not to come to the hospital.
No.
There was no way, because this was-- there were no contacts for civilians, any kind, certainly not for Jews, so we couldn't contact.
And we didn't know, I think, what happened to our mother.
I-- I don't know actually to this day.
I-- I assume she did not manage to-- because my father was not there-- and I always accuse myself that I was sick.
If it were not for me, he would have stayed home.
They would have probably both run away to Russia.
From my hometown, it was still possible.
But since he was not there, I assume that-- there is not a witness who could say one way or another.

We were in-- we tried to feed ourselves, which was not easy, because in the food lines, Poles would very often point out to the Jews in the line so that the Jews would be arrested and they would advance in the line.
So it was not so easy.

But then we heard from friends, the telephone or going through the backyards, one house to another, that there is a possibility to get out of town and to work in Baltoji Voke, or in Polish Biala-Waka, peat mine.
So we went there and we worked for a while.
I don't remember the date.
I don't remember the date.
I would say probably the end of-- certainly the end of July, not later, maybe even earlier.
We got ourselves organized.
I mean, we were there at the peat mine.
And we had some contacts with some friends.
And we decided that being in the area which is under the Lithuanian-- in Lithuanian district is not so good, because so many Lithuanians were infested with anit-Semitism at that time.

And we wanted to go further south, where we heard from farmers that the Jews in places like Dzieveniskes, Kalelishok, Varenavas, and others were still everybody in his home and the Germans didn't touch them.
And there were no Lithuanians.
There were mainly some local self-defense going, but not much.
So we-- we decided to go south.
And after a few weeks at the peat mine, or maybe more than a few weeks, maybe-- maybe a month or so, we found-- a group of us, about 15, hired a truck.
And we took us south.
And that itself was not an easy thing, because Jews were not supposed to hire trucks, and certainly not move from one place to another.
And we simply left the place, which afterwards was abandoned anyway and the people were sent back to Vilna.
The-- the peat mine was-- it belonged to-- it was occupied, so according to the laws of occupation, it belonged to the occupying Germans.
It was regarded Lithuanian.
It-- it was run by OT, that means Organisation Todt, the Todt organization.
And they were doing some earth-moving and other mine work for the Germans in the east.
I was-- then I worked most of the time cutting the peat.
But then they-- they discovered they needed someone who spoke Lithuanian and German and Polish, which I did.
So I believe that I managed to be maybe a week in the office, where I heard a little more as to what was going on, what was going to happen.
And that was one of the reasons why we ran away.
We went to a place called Kalelishok.
K-A-L-E-S-H-I-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --shok, S-H-O-K.
Kalelishok.
There we stayed for a few weeks with some Jewish families.
And at that time, the Jews were told that they have to move to Dzieveniskes, D-Z-I-E-V-E-N-I-S-K-E-S, Dzieveniskes.
And we went to Dzieveniskes.
And from Dzieveniskes, apparently the Germans received instructions to concentrate the-- the Jews from the surrounding areas in bigger towns, major sort of central provincetowns.
So we were brought to Varenavas, or in Yiddish [YIDDISH].
V-A-R-E-N-A-V-A-S.
Probably-- I'm trying to think.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Probably in January or February.
No.
My-- my father stayed in town.
Then the-- the Judenrat asked for 30 to 40 people, young people, to go out to work in the vicinity, 10 kilometers, in the-- with the forester.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I had a discussion with my father.
I-- of course I wanted to go.
But I wasn't-- I didn't want to leave my father behind.
There was no way I could take my father with me, because the age was very-- the age group was very clear, up to 30 or so.
And I went with his blessing, but probably with broken heart, I assume.
And the same was true about me.
And I went to the forester.
And there I worked in the office, mainly.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Until [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Germans surrounded Varenavas.
And we heard about it, so we immediately ran away.
And that was--
The 24th day of [NON-ENGLISH], which I believe conforms to the 28th of April, 1942.
That's when my father was killed together with the rest of the Jews of Varenavas, Dzieveniskes, Kalelishok, and some refugees like ourselves from Lithuania.
This date has to be checked.

Again, there were-- since it was still a-- a Lithuanian kind of a-- district, maybe the local people were mainly white Russians and Poles, but there were-- there was a Lithuanian element and Lithuanians were in charge.
So they needed someone who speaks Lithuanian.
And I spoke Lithuanian and German and Polish, so I-- all the languages that they needed in the office.
They were-- first of all, not all of them were keen to see the Jews killed.
Some had remorse.
Some were afraid what happened-- what will happen to them after the Russians come back, because in '42, some of them already started to doubt the permanency of the Thousand-Year Reich.
And they wanted to-- to say a good word.
And actually, they were simply repeating what they heard.
Forester.
Very little.
We heard from some people who heard from some people that they had a-- a radio that could pick up BBC shortwaves.
That was the main source of information, and it was never firsthand.
I believe so, yes.
I believe so.
But that was, I believe, in November--
Oh.
December of '42.
In December of '41.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yes.
It was-- I also did some translations for-- for a Polish landowner of the-- the German press that was available and the Lithuanian press.
So it was-- it was mentioned, of course, that-- that America sort of is falling apart, that it was bombed very severely.
It was caught completely by surprise by the Japanese and so on.
Oh, I knew from history and from First World War that America's entry would be a very important change.
It would probably sway the war quicker towards defeat of Germany.
The problem was only whether we will survive it.
It was too long.
We knew that the Allies were far away.
It was before D-Day.
It was before the big German defeats, you know, in-- in Stalingrad.
It was before all that.
But we knew very well that if America-- and we knew exactly what America meant, American power, the American industry, the American economy, the American organization.
We thought that by sheer power they will overcome Hitler.
Nobody took the German army lightly.

But I remember when I watched them through the hospital windows, I saw the Germans on the 24th of June, two days after the start of the war in Vilna, how they stripped and immediately washed-- everybody washed half his body in cold water, you know, and so on.
Although it was summer, but I saw they were strong, young, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very, very kind of-- they looked kind of a determined branch-- bunch.
I-- I thought that it won't be easy.
Practically none.
But there was an officer, I believe, called Willach who was an Austrian and was a decent guy, I think.
Anyway, he-- he was a [NON-ENGLISH], an economic officer.
He was not SS or SR.
He dealt with the economic-- like he had to deliver wood and strategic materials.
That was his job.
He was a man of about 50, and a Catholic from Austria, I think.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Because he did not make fun of anybody in the office or did not take out the Jews to be shot at random without any instructions, because there are some people in other parts that did that.
Anyway, this was quite a distant place and very few people came there as long as we delivered the wood on time.
Well, I was wandering around trying to exist, trying to find contacts with the partisans.
That-- that was not easy.
They were about 80, 90 kilometers maybe away.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was without weapons, so the chance of being accepted was minimal.
We didn't know about the Bielski brigade and others, that Jews would come.
So somehow I had a little money.
The money was in terms of-- these I did when the Russians came.
A shoemaker made in the boots, you know, he put in some golden coins that I gave him.
And I changed it gradually.
And this is what I existed on.
And of course, also some farmers had mercy in some cases.
And when we were in the office, we received some food.
We had our own kitchen.
I was-- I was wandering around.
And I decided-- in the meantime I heard from farmers who didn't know I was Jewish.
I spoke to them as if I was a Lithuanian youth escaping from the Nazis who wanted to send me to forced labor in Germany.
And the way I spoke, they could not recognize I was not Jewish, because their Lithuanian was not as pure as mine.
I spoke with a northwestern accent, [NON-ENGLISH], which is practically impossible to-- to copy.
And I-- I found out that it was quiet in Vilna ghetto.
After many murders and so on, it was quiet.

And since I had no other place-- and there was also I had a-- an unexpected meeting with a young girl who used to be a waitress in Vilna in [INAUDIBLE] restaurant, where my Lithuanian co-workers and I used to eat lunch.
It was a dairy restaurant, very good, called [NON-ENGLISH].
And she saw me.
And I wasn't sure whether she knew that-- she recognized me and I spoke to her.

But I wasn't sure whether she knew that I was Jewish, because I used to come to this restaurant during the Lithuanian rule and afterward during the Russian, with a bunch of 10 Lithuanians, men and women.
And we all worked for the passport department and then for others.
So she- I wasn't sure whether she knew I was Jewish.
No.
I don't recall her name.
Somewhere about 70, 80 kilometers from Vilna.
Not--
No, not only she, but farmers who-- with whom I traded some.
I bought some bread and I paid them with a local currency and so on, or with something else.
So I heard from the farmers, who said-- some of them said, oh, we were in Vilna.
We went out near the ghetto.
Nothing special.
There is a-- there is a gate.
There is a Lithuanian policeman and a-- a ghetto policeman.
But people come and go and so on.
That's the way they described it.
Of course, I took everything with a grain of salt.
But even so, since I had no other place and I had to get away, because I was afraid that she might know that I'm Jewish.
So I decided to go back to Vilna.
The 13th of July, 1942.
I went to the southern part of town, because I came from the south.
And I was looking on the main highways for Jewish brigades.
I was told by the people that there were some people working in groups, brigades, in various organized workplaces.
So I just walked and mingled among the people.
And walked-- somebody hang a-- a yellow sign on my back, some one of the people that worked.
And I walked in together with them.
I contacted my colleagues.
My friends from Lithuania.
There were various people.
And they immediately fixed me up to get administration.
And I-- they registered me.
They gave me a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shine-- I mean, a for-- for food-- what do you call it?
A ration book, yes.
A ration book.
However meager, it was important, because that registered you.
And I had a document.
And then I started to make plans to get away.
I-- I had kind of a permit, yes.
And I worked in the ghetto in the Social Affairs Committee, which actually distributed food to the needy.
Now, the food did not come from heaven.
It was confiscated by the ghetto police from Jews returning from their work if they brought in excessive amounts.
So sometimes, especially if a Lithuanian or a German policeman watched, they used to take away the food, put it aside, and even beat up the person.
And they used to bring it to the ghetto.
And then we used to read the requests in writing of people who wanted.
And we used to decide two kilograms of potatoes, one kilogram of bread, two kilograms of bread, some soup cards, and so on.
And this is what we did.

The-- this committee was of course a place where some of the FPO operatives, like Sonja Madeisker and Rosa [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE]-- the first two are Communists and the one was from the Labor Zionists.
They worked.
There was also [INAUDIBLE], who was a Buddhist.
He was also a member of the committee.
And I knew about them, but I was very careful to keep my nose clean and not to say anything about it.
The FPO was Fareynikte Partizaner Organizatsye, which means in English United Partisan Organization.
And of course, it was a open secret in Vilna and they knew about it.
Nobody suggested to me to join, maybe because I-- I voiced opinions against the United Organization with the Communists.

I did not believe that joining with the Communists, although it was meant because people had the idea that Communists must have some very good contacts in town, outside the ghetto and-- But I knew the Lithuanians from childhood very well.
And I knew the Lithuanian Communist Party is composed of 50% Nazi agents.
And I was right.
And of course, the man who was former mayor of Vilna, Vitus, who was a Lithuanian Communist, and others, they were immediately captured.
They were compromised.
And in the end, they gave the name of Wittenberg as an example.
And then the whole tragedy of Wittenberg started.
And the foundations of PLO were shaken.
I lived on Spitalna, I believe number 9.
I had a room about seven feet by nine feet.
It is kind of a dilapidated thing.
I don't know.
It was overlooked.
And then somebody-- I found it.
And they agreed, the ghetto administration, that I should live there.
I'm not quite sure how many there were left.
Vilna had about 80,000 Jews.
And there were two ghettos.
One was destroyed.
The other one was sort of cut down to-- to small.
So I believe that must have been about 26,000 or so.
The cultural activities were-- first of all, the ghetto administration was in favor of these activities.

The Zionist youth groups, of course, in order to continue their spiritual affiliation with the Jewish people, were also very much interested, and also to give people who were always tense-- to give them an opportunity to relax a little.
Some of the activities, well, it was Hebrew theater.
There was a choir.
I participated in both.
There were other activities.
There were libraries.
There were books.
There was a secret yeshiva, a small yeshiva.
That means people studied the--
